claim_chain: Only 43% of menu items met all reduction targets, indicating low adherence
Heterogeneity observed across food categories and restaurants
Similar restaurants vary in adherence, suggesting improvement is possible without changing menu focus
Findings indicate voluntary schemes have low adherence
Mandatory regulations may be more effective

typical_pattern: State main finding with quantitative evidence
Interpret implications for policy or practice

likely_priorities: Sales-weighted analysis rather than item-level counts
Temporal trends rather than single time point
Complete menu coverage across all restaurants
likely_attacks: Cannot account for heterogeneity in item-level sales
Healthier items may represent smaller proportion of actual sales
Manual data collection limited sample size
Reliance on restaurants' own reporting for accuracy
Not all menu items included for each company
Single time point prevents determination of trends
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The discussion explicitly states limitations including: inability to account for heterogeneity in item-level sales due to lack of accessible sales data, noting that healthier items may be responsible for smaller proportion of sales; time and resource constraints of manual data collection limiting sample size; dependence on restaurants' own reporting for completeness and accuracy; use of subcategory average serving sizes where data missing; not all menu items included for each company; assessment at only one time point preventing determination of trends. The paper also notes that technical guidance highlights sales-weighted averages as gold standard but not always possible for OOH sector.
